0:0.rendering: These are the names of the sons of Israel who came to Egypt with Jacob, each with his household:
0:0.note: The Hebrew title of this book is simply "These are the names" (we'elleh shemot), echoing the very first words of Genesis where God names the light, the sky, and the dry land. This opening line stitches the family story of Genesis, the seventy souls who went down to Egypt, directly into the new chapter of Israel's explosive growth, setting the stage for the God who calls people by name and turns a small clan into a vast multitude that will one day fill the earth.

0:1.note: This opening list of Jacob's sons in Exodus 1:2 deliberately echoes the family roster that closed Genesis, signaling that the story of Israel's explosive growth in Egypt is the direct continuation of the covenant family God shaped through Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob.

0:2.note: This snippet of names in the opening genealogy of Exodus is a quiet echo of the promise to Abraham that his descendants would become a great multitude, now bursting into a people so numerous that Pharaoh feels threatened. The list of Jacob's sons reminds us how the fractured family from Genesis has grown into a nation in Egypt, setting the stage for the great deliverance that will reveal God's covenant faithfulness and his desire to dwell among his image-bearing people.

0:3.rendering: Reuben, Simeon, Levi, and Judah; Issachar, Zebulun, and Benjamin; Dan and Naphtali; Gad and Asher.
0:3.note: This list of Jacob's twelve sons reappears at pivotal moments in the biblical story, acting like a family tree that keeps the promise of a vast, multi-tribal people alive. Right after naming them, Exodus tells us they became fruitful and multiplied, turning a small clan into a nation, which echoes God's original blessing to fill the earth and previews how this people will become the vessel through which blessing returns to all nations, ultimately fulfilled in Jesus, the true Israelite.

0:4.rendering: All the people who came from Jacob's body numbered seventy. But Joseph was already in Egypt.
0:4.note: The number seventy here echoes the table of nations in Genesis 10, where seventy peoples spread out across the earth. Now Jacob's seventy descendants descend into Egypt, setting the stage for a new chapter in the story of how God will form one family from many and bring blessing to all the scattered nations.

0:5.rendering: Joseph died, and all his brothers, and that whole generation.
0:5.note: This short verse marks the end of one era and the beginning of another in Israel's story. It echoes the language of Genesis where the death of a generation often signals a shift from promise to threat, setting the stage for the Israelites' explosive growth and eventual oppression, themes of exile and return that run through the whole biblical narrative.

0:6.rendering: But the sons of Israel were fruitful and swarmed and multiplied and became very very strong, so that the land was filled with them.
0:6.note: This verse echoes the creation mandate in Genesis 1 where God blesses living creatures to "be fruitful and multiply and fill" the earth, now applied to Abraham's family in Egypt. The repeated verbs highlight how God's promise of countless offspring is bursting into reality, setting the stage for the covenant story that will ultimately fill the whole earth with image-bearers through Jesus.

0:7.rendering: A new king rose to power over Egypt, who did not know Joseph.
0:7.note: The Hebrew word for "knew" (yada) here carries the sense of covenant loyalty and relationship, not just information. This new king launches the long pattern of oppression and exile in the Bible, setting the stage for God to rescue his people and reveal himself as the faithful king who remembers his covenant.

0:8.rendering: Then he said to his people, "Look, the Israelite people are more numerous and stronger than we are."
0:8.note: This moment echoes the promise God made to Abraham that his descendants would become a great nation, yet here that very fruitfulness triggers fear in Egypt, setting the stage for oppression. The Hebrew word for "numerous" (rav) links back to the creation blessing to "be fruitful and multiply," showing how the image-of-God family is both a threat to empire and the beginning of God's rescue story that will ultimately lead to a new kind of kingdom in Jesus.

0:9.rendering: Come, let us deal shrewdly with them, or they will multiply even more, and when war breaks out, they will join our enemies, fight against us, and leave the land.
0:9.note: The word for "deal shrewdly" here is the same Hebrew root (hakam) that will later describe the wisdom of Joseph and the serpent in Eden, but now it's twisted into fear-driven cunning. This moment launches the long biblical pattern of empires trying to suppress God's image-bearing people through oppression and control, a thread that runs from Pharaoh's brick quotas to the later exiles and ultimately finds its resolution when Jesus, the true image of God, leads a new exodus that no empire can stop.

0:10.rendering: So they appointed taskmasters over them to oppress them with forced labor. And they built supply cities for Pharaoh: Pithom and Rameses.
0:10.note: The word for "oppress" here echoes the same Hebrew term used in Genesis for the pain of childbirth and the toil of the cursed ground, showing how Egypt has turned the image-of-God people into slaves who build store-cities that ironically mirror the tower-building project of Babel. This sets the stage for the Bible's great pattern of slavery and deliverance that will reach its climax when Jesus frees humanity from a deeper bondage.

0:11.rendering: But the more they oppressed them, the more they multiplied and spread out, so that the Egyptians came to dread the sons of Israel.
0:11.note: This little verse captures a delicious irony in the Exodus story: the word for "multiplied" is the same one God used in his original blessing to humanity and to Israel, showing how the Creator's life-giving power stubbornly pushes back against empire's attempts to control and diminish image-bearers. It's the beginning of a pattern we'll see again and again, where oppression and exile only set the stage for God's surprising deliverance.

0:12.rendering: The Egyptians made the Israelites work ruthlessly. They made their lives bitter with harsh labor, making mortar and bricks and every kind of work in the fields. In all their work they worked them ruthlessly.
0:12.note: This scene of ruthless exploitation echoes the ancient curse of Genesis 3 where humanity's work turns painful and the ground itself resists, but here it is inflicted by an empire that refuses to know the God of the oppressed. The Hebrew word for "ruthlessly" (beparek) will later appear in Leviticus to describe how God commands Israel never to treat one another this way, revealing a God who enters the story to reverse the very pattern of human slavery and restore creation's original blessing.

0:13.rendering: They made their lives bitter with hard service, in mortar and brick, and in all kinds of work in the field. They imposed on them all their work with ruthless harshness.
0:13.note: The word for "bitter" here (marar) echoes the name "Marah," the bitter waters Israel would later encounter after escaping Egypt, and it sets up a pattern of bitterness that runs through the biblical story: from slavery's harsh labor to the groaning of creation itself under curse. This ruthless exploitation of image-bearers in Egypt becomes the dark backdrop against which God hears, remembers, and acts to create a new people who will one day bring blessing to the whole world, a thread that reaches its climax when Jesus enters our slavery to sin and death.

0:14.rendering: The king of Egypt spoke to the Hebrew midwives, one named Shiphrah and the second named Puah.
0:14.note: The names Shiphrah (meaning "beautiful one" or "to be pleasing") and Puah (possibly "splendor" or "to cry out") are the first two personal names mentioned in Exodus after the opening genealogy. This tiny detail quietly signals that these two women will play a key role in resisting Pharaoh's empire of death, a theme that echoes throughout the biblical story as God consistently chooses the overlooked to protect his image-bearing people and advance his purposes.

0:15.rendering: He said, "When you help the Hebrew women give birth and observe them on the birthstool, if it is a son, then you shall put him to death, but if it is a daughter, then she shall live."
0:15.note: This chilling command from Egypt's king launches a theme that runs through the whole biblical story: empires of fear trying to snuff out the promised seed, only for God to preserve life through the courage of women and the weakness of babies. It echoes the curse on the serpent in Genesis 3, setting the stage for the exodus deliverance that will one day point forward to Jesus, the ultimate child the powers of this world could not destroy.

0:16.rendering: But the midwives feared God and did not do as the king of Egypt commanded them. Instead, they let the boys live.
0:16.note: The Hebrew word for "feared" here is the same one used throughout the Bible for the proper response to God, a deep awe that leads to faithful action, and it's striking that these two women defy Pharaoh because their fear of God outweighs their fear of the most powerful man on earth. This moment echoes the larger biblical pattern where God works through the unlikely and powerless to preserve his covenant people, a thread that runs from the midwives' courage all the way to Jesus, the ultimate deliverer born under another tyrant's threat.

0:17.rendering: The king of Egypt summoned the midwives and said to them, "Why have you done this thing, letting the boys live?"
0:17.note: The Hebrew word for "live" here (chayah) echoes God's original command to humanity to "be fruitful and multiply and fill the earth" back in Genesis 1, a blessing the midwives are now protecting even as Pharaoh tries to suppress it. This sets up the Exodus story as a new act of creation, where heaven and earth collide as God raises up deliverers to rescue his image-bearing people from imperial oppression.

0:18.rendering: The midwives said to Pharaoh, "Because the Hebrew women are not like the Egyptian women, for they are vigorous and give birth before the midwife comes to them."
0:18.note: The Hebrew word for "vigorous" (chayot) literally means "alive" or "lively like animals," painting these fearless midwives as full of life in the face of death. This small act of civil disobedience in Egypt echoes the larger biblical pattern of God using unlikely deliverers to rescue his people from imperial power, a thread that runs from midwives to Moses and ultimately finds its climax in Jesus, the true deliverer who brings life out of oppression.

0:19.rendering: So God dealt well with the midwives. And the people multiplied and grew very strong.
0:19.note: The Hebrew word for "dealt well" carries the idea of making something good or beneficial, echoing how God brings goodness out of chaos in Genesis 1. Even under Pharaoh's oppressive empire, God's blessing on these two courageous women causes his image-bearing people to multiply and fill the land, a pattern of fruitful life breaking through the threat of death that will echo all the way to the birth of a new kind of deliverer.

0:20.rendering: And because the midwives feared God, he made households for them.
0:20.note: The Hebrew word for "made households" is the same one used when God builds Eve as a partner for Adam and when he establishes Israel as a people. These two women, by protecting life at great personal risk, become the first in Exodus to experience God's covenant faithfulness, a small but powerful echo of how God creates family and future for those who align with his purposes.

0:21.rendering: Then Pharaoh commanded all his people: "Every son who is born to the Hebrews you shall throw into the Nile, but every daughter you shall let live."
0:21.note: This chilling decree escalates Egypt's fear-driven oppression, turning the river that symbolized life and abundance into an instrument of death for Israel's sons, echoing the earlier threat against baby Moses. In the larger biblical story it sets the stage for God's dramatic rescue through the waters, a pattern of watery deliverance that runs from the flood, through the Red Sea, and ultimately points forward to baptism and new life in Jesus.
